perturbation.original_text: Should any legal proceedings be commenced to secure or enforce any right under this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover from the non-prevailing party its reasonable attorneys' fees and costs, in addition to all other relief to which the prevailing party may be entitled at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: Should any legal proceedings be commenced to secure or enforce any right under this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover costs, in addition to all other relief to which the prevailing party may be entitled at law or in equity.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'reasonable attorneys' fees' it may introduce ambiguity to what costs are recoverable. Some laws require specification when there is attorney reimbursement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 57.01 dictates how costs can be awarded. By not specifying 'reasonable attorneys' fees', enforceability is reduced and could lead to a disagreement on cost recovery, going against the spirit of predictable cost awards.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies 'reasonable attorneys' fees,' while the changed text only mentions 'costs.' Rule 57.01 on costs of proceedings from Scraped Law Snippet 1 suggests a framework for cost awards, but doesn't explicitly address attorney's fees. The removal of 'reasonable attorneys' fees' introduces ambiguity, potentially leading to disputes about whether attorney's fees are recoverable as part of 'costs.' This ambiguity undermines the predictability of cost awards, creating a structural flaw in the contract's enforceability, leading to a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice required or permitted u nder this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be delivered and deemed received as follows: (i) by personal delivery or by facsimile (with electronic confirmation) during the recipient’s normal business hours, in which case it shall be deemed received at the time of such delivery or facsimile as the case may be; (ii) by certified or registered mail, in which case it shall be deemed received on the date such return receipt is signed; or (iii) by prepaid nationally recognized overnight courier by next day delivery, in which case it shall be deemed received on the day following the date of deposit with such courier.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice required or permitted u nder this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be delivered and deemed received as follows: (i) by personal delivery; (ii) by certified or registered mail; or (iii) by prepaid nationally recognized overnight courier by next day delivery.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific details of when notices are deemed received can cause ambiguity and conflict with Ontario's Electronic Commerce Act
perturbation.law_citation: Electronic Commerce Act, 2000, S.O. 2000, c. 17, s. 22
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 22 of the Electronic Commerce Act sets rules around when an electronic document is sent and received. Omitting detail when the delivery is considered valid leads to potential dispute and clashes with the E-Commerce Act.

perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly defines how notices are deemed received for different delivery methods, while the changed text removes these specifics. The Law Explanation points out that Section 22 of the Electronic Commerce Act sets rules around when an electronic document is sent and received. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references this Act and its contents, including provisions related to the time of sending of electronic information or documents. By removing the specific details of when notices are considered received, the modified text creates ambiguity and a potential conflict with the principles outlined in the Electronic Commerce Act, potentially leading to disputes over when a notice is legally considered to have been delivered. The Perturbation Explanation states the removal of these details can cause conflict with this Act, supporting the existence of a legal contradiction due to the ambiguity introduced.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement supersedes all prior and contemporaneous negotiations, agreements, discussions and writings and constitutes the entire Agreement between the parties with respect to subject matter hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire Agreement between the parties with respect to subject matter hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'supersedes all prior and contemporaneous negotiations, agreements, discussions and writings' reduces clarity and may conflict with the Parol Evidence Rule.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ontario Evidence Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. E.23
perturbation.law_citation: Ontario Evidence Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. E.23, s. 91
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 91 allows judges to admit parol evidence. Removing the superseding clause makes it less clear that any prior agreements are not part of the overall agreement which goes against contract law.

Attendance of witnesses 20. Examination of witnesses, proof of contradictory wr
perturbation.justification: The removal of 'supersedes all prior and contemporaneous negotiations, agreements, discussions and writings' weakens the contract's ability to prevent the introduction of parol evidence, potentially contradicting established contract law principles. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1, mentions 'Evidence Act' indicating the importance of evidence consideration in legal proceedings. Omitting the clause increases ambiguity regarding prior agreements, creating a structural flaw that contradicts the intent to have a complete and clear contract. This aligns with the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition, where clarity and enforceability are affected.
